did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the transformer-based deep learning model SegFormer (specifically segFormer-b0) to perform semantic segmentation of H&E-stained whole slide images for skin tissue analysis, including skin layers and carcinomas, achieving an overall accuracy of 0.875.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is published in 'Data in Brief' as a 'Data Article' presenting the 'Histo-Seg' dataset of 38 annotated H&E-stained whole slide images. It includes original experimental methods for data collection (biopsies, staining, imaging), annotation protocols, and validation results using the SegFormer model (overall accuracy 0.875), which constitutes original research producing new data and technical validation rather than a literature review.

title: Hematoxylin and Eosin-stained whole slide image dataset annotated for skin tissue segmentation
brief_summary: This study introduces the Histo-Seg dataset, comprising 38 high-resolution Hematoxylin and Eosin-stained whole slide images annotated for 12 classes including skin layers, tissues, and carcinomas. The dataset was collected from biopsies of 14 patients and processed using brightfield microscopy at 20x magnification. To validate the annotations, the authors trained a transformer-based SegFormer-b0 model for semantic segmentation. The model achieved an overall accuracy of 0.875 on the Histo-Seg dataset, with layer-wise accuracies exceeding 0.75. This resource aims to facilitate the development and benchmarking of automated diagnostic systems for skin disease analysis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 12 annotated classes in the Histo-Seg dataset, used for semantic segmentation validation.
One of the 12 annotated classes in the Histo-Seg dataset, used for semantic segmentation validation.

all_task_types.target: Pixel-level annotations for 12 discrete classes including skin layers (epidermis, dermis, hypodermis), tissues (glands, follicles, inflammation, keratin), and carcinomas (BCC, SCC, IEC).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'semantic segmentation' of H&E-stained whole slide images. The model (SegFormer-b0) predicts pixel-wise class labels for 12 distinct tissue structures and disease regions, validated by an overall accuracy of 0.875.

datasets_used.name: Histo-Seg
datasets_used.description: A dataset comprising 38 high-resolution Hematoxylin and Eosin-stained whole slide images (WSIs) paired with ground truth segmentation masks. The images were collected from skin biopsies of 14 patients (11 males, 3 females, aged 36-81) via Non-Invasive Diagnostic Innovations (NIDI) in Skin (USA) and processed at the National University of Sciences and Technology. Images range from 0.22 to 1.7 Gigapixels, captured at 20x magnification using a HEIDSTAR HDS-MS-200A brightfield microscope.

use.training: Used to train the SegFormer-b0 model for semantic segmentation validation.
use.validation: Used to validate the model's segmentation accuracy (overall accuracy 0.875).
use.other: Published as a public benchmarking resource for automated skin tissue segmentation.

datasets_used.name: Queensland dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available histopathology dataset annotated for the same 12 classes as Histo-Seg, used for external comparison and validation of the segmentation model.

use.training: Used to train the SegFormer-b0 model for comparative validation.
use.validation: Used to compare segmentation results against the Histo-Seg dataset.
use.other: Served as an external benchmark for validating the correctness of the Histo-Seg annotations.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper reports descriptive accuracy metrics (overall accuracy 0.875, layer-wise accuracy > 0.75) to validate the dataset annotations.

Histo-Seg.notes: The model was trained using 50 epochs, a learning rate of 1 × 10–5, and the Adam optimizer. Layer-wise accuracy was observed to be > 0.75 for each layer separately.

Queensland dataset.notes: Used for external comparison and validation of the segmentation model against the Histo-Seg dataset.

architecture_used.input_size: Not reported (processed via patching to reduce memory requirements)

architecture_details.mechanism: Attention matrix computation and key-value mechanism

preprocessing_techniques: Patching of whole slide images to reduce memory requirements
De-identification of patient data to ensure privacy
Anonymization of data to prevent direct or indirect identification of individuals
conclusions: The Histo-Seg dataset, comprising 38 annotated H&E-stained whole slide images, serves as a valuable resource for benchmarking and developing automated semantic segmentation models for skin tissue analysis.
Validation using the SegFormer-b0 transformer model demonstrated the correctness of the dataset annotations, achieving an overall accuracy of 0.875 and layer-wise accuracies exceeding 0.75.

The high-resolution nature of the dataset (captured at 20x magnification) necessitates specialized computational strategies, such as patch-based analysis, to manage substantial memory requirements during processing.
The dataset facilitates the standardization of results and reproducibility in computational pathology research, aiding in the early detection and diagnosis of skin diseases.
Future work should include datasets acquired at different magnifications (e.g., 5x and 10x) to support in-depth analysis and the development of magnification-agnostic models.
important_remarks: The dataset is small (38 WSIs from 14 patients), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the validation results.
The study population is heavily skewed towards males (11 males vs. 3 females), introducing potential gender bias in the dataset.
All images were acquired at a single magnification (20x), limiting the model's ability to generalize to other magnifications; the authors explicitly note this as a limitation.
The validation relies on a single model (SegFormer-b0) and a single metric (accuracy), which may not fully capture segmentation performance nuances compared to metrics like Dice or IoU.
The dataset is restricted to non-commercial use (CC BY-NC license), which may hinder certain clinical deployment or commercial AI development applications.

Annotation quality was validated via a double-annotation protocol on only 20% of the dataset, with discrepancies resolved by pathologists, rather than reporting inter-annotator agreement statistics for the full set.

The external validation used the Queensland dataset, but the paper does not report detailed per-class metrics for this external set, only overall and layer-wise accuracy, making direct comparison difficult.

The high resolution of the WSIs (up to 1.7 Gigapixels) necessitates patch-based processing, which introduces potential issues with context loss at patch boundaries that are not explicitly addressed in the validation.
No statistical hypothesis testing was performed; results are presented as descriptive accuracy metrics without confidence intervals or p-values.
